coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of catalytically active multielement-doped carbon dots and application for colorimetric detection of glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bin
Liu, Feng
Wu, Yuanya
Chen, Yanfen
Weng, Bo
Li, Chang Ming
coredata.dc:description: A novel multielement-doped carbon dots (ME-CDs) were synthesized by simply hydrothermal treatment of animal blood. Results revealed that the prepared carbon dots were mainly constituted of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements with trace amount of iron and sulfur elements, illustrating strong fluorescence with a quantum yield of 32.6%. The as-prepared carbon dots displayed excellent peroxidase-like catalytic activities attributed to the doped iron elements in the ME-CDs. The nanodots could be utilized to fabricate a colorimetric sensor for glucose detection and demonstrated good sensitivity and specificity. This work provides a facile strategy for the synthesis of catalytically active carbon dots and building of colorimetric sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Blood
Glucose
Colorimetric sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The peroxidase-like activity of the ME-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Fundamental Research Funds ( XDJK2016C087 ) for the Central Universities and PhD start-up grants SWU113112 from Southwest University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The mechanism of the colorimetric glucose biosensor.
TEM image and size distribution (inset) (A), wide-angle XRD pattern (B), Raman spectrum (C) and EDS pattern (D) of the synthesized ME–CDs.

(a) XPS spectra of the blood, ME-CDs, and carbon residues (atom percentage of S, C, N and O elements is marked). (b)–(e) High-resolution XPS spectra of O1s, C1s, N1s, and S2p in the ME-CDs; (f) FTIR spectra of the ME-CDs.

(a) UV/Vis absorption and fluorescent spectra of the obtained ME-CDs (excitation wavelength, 365nm). Inset: Photograph of the obtained ME-CDs under illumination of white light (left) and UV (365nm) light (right); (b) The excitation-dependent PL behaviors of the nanodots.

(a) UV–vis spectra of the ME-CDs (I), TMB+H2O2 (II) and TMB+H2O2 +ME-CDs (III) aqueous solution in pH 4.5 acetate buffer. (b) UV–vis spectra of the TMB+H2O2 system in the presence of B-CDs (IV), C-CDs (V), P-CDs (VI), Fe-CDs (VII) and H-CDs (VIII) (in 20mM, pH 4.5, NaAc/HAc buffer solution. (The concentration TMB and H2O2 were 0.1mM and 1.3mM, respectively; the concentration of ME-CDs, B-CDs, C-CDs, P-CDs, Fe-CDs and H-CDs were 0.01mg/mL). Inset: The corresponding photographs.
UV/Vis absorption spectra of the ME-CDs+TMB (a), glucose+ME-CDs+TMB (b), and upon the addition of glucose that was pretreated with GOx (c) and corresponding photograph (inset).

A: UV/Vis absorption spectra of the glucose-GOx/TMB/ME-CDs system in the presence of different concentration of glucose; B: photographs of the system with glucose concentration of 0.00, 0.20, 0.40, 0.60, 0.80, 1.20, 1.70, 2.00, and 2.50mM, respectively. C: Calibration curves for glucose determination. All measurements were performed in 20mM HAc/NaAc buffer solutions (pH 4.5) containing 0.8mM glucose, 0.1mM TMB, and 2μg/mL ME-CDs. (Error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements).

A: Selectivity of glucose detection (Inset: The corresponding photographs), the concentration of glucose and interferents was 1.2mM; B: Glucose in human serum (Inset: the corresponding photograohs and glucose concentration of three samples). All measurements were performed in 0.1M HAc/NaAc buffer solutions (pH 4.5) containing 1.2mM glucose, 0.1mM TMB, and 10μg/mL ME-CDs. (Error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements).
Detection of glucose concentration in human serum by the method and handheld glucose meter.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of catalytically active multielement-doped carbon dots and application for colorimetric detection of glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Faculty of Materials & Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Faculty of Materials & Energy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel multielement-doped carbon dots (ME-CDs) were synthesized by simply hydrothermal treatment of animal blood. Results revealed that the prepared carbon dots were mainly constituted of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements with trace amount of iron and sulfur elements, illustrating strong fluorescence with a quantum yield of 32.6%. The as-prepared carbon dots displayed excellent peroxidase-like catalytic activities attributed to the doped iron elements in the ME-CDs. The nanodots could be utilized to fabricate a colorimetric sensor for glucose detection and demonstrated good sensitivity and specificity. This work provides a facile strategy for the synthesis of catalytically active carbon dots and building of colorimetric sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Catalytically active nanomaterials can perform similarly as biological enzymes in chemical and biological reactions [1]. To date, a variety of nanomaterials such as gold nanoparticles, Fe3O4, CeO2, MoS2, WS2, Co3O4, graphene oxide, and carbon nanotubes, have been verified possessing intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic properties and used in various applications including bioanalysis [2–9]. Compared to biological enzymes, nanomaterials displayed more advantages such as low cost, good chemical stability, active catalytic activity, good recovery and recycling ability [10]. Thus catalytically active nanoparticles has attracted increasing attentions recently.

Carbon dots (CDs), as novel fluorescent nanomateials, have been widely studied and applied in optoelectronics, photocatalysis, medical diagnosis and biosensors, due to their superior optical properties, good chemical stability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [11–14]. Since Huang and coworkers reported that C-dots prepared from candle soot could serve as peroxidase mimetics, a new field for the application of C-dots has been opened up [15]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been reported to have the ability to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to generate HO [16]. And the functions of the oxygen functional groups on the surface of GQDs in catalytic peroxidase-like reactions are investigated. It was verified that the ketonic carbonyl groups (CO) acted as the catalytically active sites of GQDs, the carboxylic groups (OCO) were substrate binding sites, whereas the hydroxy groups (COH) would inhibit the catalytic reaction. Chen and coworkers [17] studied the catalytic active of nitrogen-doped GQDs and suggested that the N atoms on benzene ring atoms influenced the spin density and the charge distribution of the carbon atoms, facilitating catalytic reactions. In addition, the nitrogen-doped C-dots (N-CDs) prepared by refluxing carbonized β-cyclodextrin in nitric acid were also reported to have catalytic ability due to the existence of graphitic carbon in the CDs [18].

In this work, we synthesized a novel catalytically active carbon dots using duck blood as carbon source. As is known to all, animal blood is highly specialized tissue composed of more than 4000 different kinds of components [19]. The chemical components of animal blood contained proteins, enzyme, hormones, cholesterol, nutrients and inorganic salts, which played an important role in biochemical reactions. We assume that the carbon dots prepared from animal blood could bring us a surprise due to the complex components. By hydrothermal treatment of duck blood, strong fluorescent carbon dots were obtained. These novel carbon dots were confirmed to contain carbon, oxygen, nitrogen elements and trace amount of sulfur and iron elements. The multielement-doped carbon dots (ME-CDs) displayed excellent peroxidase-like catalytic activities which were related to the doped iron species. The ME-CDs could be utilized for the preparation of colorimetric sensors which exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity to glucose.

In comparison with these reported CDs with peroxidase-like catalytic activities, the present ME-CDs synthesized with animal blood that can be obtained with low cost, and adequate supply source, suggesting that the method was suitable for large-scale preparation of catalytically active CDs.

Chitosan (CS) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in this work were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI), Protoporphyrin, Hydrogen peroxide and citric acid were bought from Aladdin Biochemical Technologies Inc, Shanghai. Glucose oxidase (GOx) was purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai,China). The duck blood was purchased from local supermarket. Deionized water (18.2MΩ.cm at 25°C) prepared by a Milli-Q water system was used throughout all experiments. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further treatment.

UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a UV2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with one pair of 10mm quartz cell. Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectrums were performed on an FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo-Fisher, Nicolet 6700) in the range of 400−4000cm−1 and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using 200W monochromated Al Kα radiation (Thermo Scientic ESCALAB 250Xi). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100) measurements were carried out using an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The samples for TEM characterization were prepared by coating the given volume of ME-CDs dispersion on a carbon-coated copper grid, and then dried under vacuum at 50°C for 24h. Fluorescent emission spectra of prepared ME-CDs were recorded by an FL spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, RF-5301PC). The reliability of the proposed method was evaluated by comparing with Bayer Contour TS Glucose meter (Bayer HealthCare LLC).

The multiple elements-doped carbon dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using duck blood as carbon precursor. In a typical synthesis, 2.0g duck blood was crushed and mixed with 10mL DI water. The mixture was then transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 10h in an oven. Then the suspension was purified via centrifugation at 8000rpm for 15min. The obtained dark brown supernatant was further purified by dialysis (1000Da dialysis bag) for 24h and then diluted with DI water and stored at 4°C as not in use.

In order to study the catalysis of the ME-CDs, several other CDs were synthesis by the same method and BPEI (2g), citric acid (2g), hemoglobin (2g), BPEI (2g) + protoporphyrin (150mg), and BPEI (2g) + FeCl3 (150mg) as carbon sources.

The QY of ME-CDs was determined using quinine sulfate (QY: 0.54 at 360nm in 0.1M H2SO4) as the standard. In order to minimize the re-absorption effects, absorbencies in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength (360nm). The QY of the ME-CDs was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2
Where Q is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and A refers to the absorbance. The subscript R refers to the corresponding parameter of known fluorescent standard.

Peroxidase-like activity of the prepared ME-CDs was evaluated by the catalytic oxidation of the peroxidase substrate, 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine sulfate (TMB). In order to examine the catalytic capability of the ME-CDs for the oxidation of TMB, 50mL of 30wt% H2O2 and 30mL of 10mM TMB were added into 3mL deionized water containing 10μg/mL ME-CDs, and absorption spectra were recorded at 25°C.

For the detection of glucose, 20μL of 0.1mg/mL GOx was mixed with variable concentration of freshly prepared glucose aqueous solution (10mM PBS solution, pH, 7.4) and incubated at 37°C for 2h. Then 10μL of TMB (2mM), 20μL of ME-CDs (0.1mM) and 150μL HAc/NaAc buffer solution (20mM, pH 4.5) were added and the absorption spectra of the reaction solutions were measured after incubating at room temperature for 30min. In order to investigate the selectivity of our strategy for glucose detection, the same experiments were carried out using other related specimens, such as fructose, maltose, lactose and sucrose to replace glucose. All determinations were repeated at least three times.

The detection of glucose in human serum was performed in the same way. Human serum was obtained from local hospital and pre-processed by centrifugation. Then 20μL of serum was mixed with 20μL of 0.1mg/mL GOx and incubated at 37°C for 2h. After addition of 10μL of TMB, 20μL of 0.1mg/mL ME-CDs and 150μL HAc/NaAc (20mM, pH 4.5) buffer solution, the mixture was kept at room temperature for 1h and the concentration of glucose was detected.

Firstly, the obtained ME-CDs were characterized by TEM. Fig. 1 showed well dispersed nanodots were successfully formed, and the corresponding particle size distribution histograms (inset) indicate that the statistical particle size of the nanodots was about 2.0–4.0nm with average diameters of 2.8nm. The high resolution image in Fig. 1 inset shows that lattice spacing of the ME-CDs was about 0.21nm, indicating in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100facet), which suggested that the prepared carbon nanodots was similar to many reported CDs. [20–22]

Then powder XRD was performed to investigate the phase and degree of crystallinity of the ME–CDs. As shown in Fig. 1B, the XRD pattern of the ME–CDs exhibited two diffraction peaks at around 23.2 and 42°, which corresponded to the planes of graphite lattice spacing (002) and (100), respectively [23]. The XRD patterns revealed the highly crystalline and graphitic structure of the synthesized ME–CDs. Raman spectra of the ME–CDs are shown in Fig. 3 C. The D band at around 1360cm−1 is attributed to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite. The peak at 1570cm−1 (G band) is related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice [24]. The spectrum of EDS in Fig. 1D depicts that the as-prepared carbon dots contains C, O, N, S, and traces of Fe elements (the EDS elemental mapping was shown in Fig. S2).

The element analysis and chemical composition of the resultant nanodots were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the obtained ME-CDs were mainly composed of three elements including carbon (66.64%), nitrogen (13.12%) and oxygen (19.86%) with binding energy of 285, 398.5 and 531eV, respectively. In addition, there were only trace amount of sulfur and iron elements with atom percentage of 0.16% and 0.06% were found in the carbon dots, which may come from the protein residues. In the high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum of the ME-CDs, two main binding energy peaks at 390.7 and 391.8eV confirmed the existent of CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 2b). In C1s XPS spectrum of the ME-CDs (Fig. 2c), the binding energy peak at 284.5eV was attributed to the graphitic structure (sp2 CC) of the ME-CDs while the peak at 285.5eV indicated from CO, CN, and CS bonds. In addition, the peak around 287.9eV suggested the presence of CO [20]. There were two main peaks at 399.7 and 400.6eV in N1s spectrum, indicating the presence of pyridinic and pyrrolic type N atoms (Fig. 2d). In the high-resolution spectrum of S2p, two peaks corresponded to CSC and CSO2- were observed. Atom percentage of XPS data indicates the existence of iron atom (0.06%), but no meaningful Fe element peak is observed in the high-resolution spectrum of Fe2p (as shown in Fig. S1).

FTIR spectra were performed to further characterize the groups in the ME-CDs. The absorption bands at 3417, 3260, and 2960cm−1 were attributed to NH, OH and CH bonds, respectively. The existence of peak at 2345cm−1 was resulted by CN and SH bonds. The peak at 1670cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching vibration. The peak at 1403 and 1458cm−1 can be assigned to as NH, and COO– groups, and the broad band at 1310cm−1 is ascribed to CN and CS bonds. The bond at 1110cm−1 is attributed to CO and COC bonds.

The optical properties of the ME-CDs were explored using UV–vis absorption and emission spectra at room temperature. Fig. 3a showed that the nanodots in aqueous solution had a strong absorption peak at 268.5nm and a weak absorption peak at around 320nm. Under UV light (365nm) excitation, bright blue luminescence could be clearly seen (inset in Fig. 3a). The emission spectrum with the maximum emission wavelength of 437nm was obtained at excitation wavelength of 365nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of the ME-CDs was calculated to be about 32.6% with the quinine sulfate as standard.

Similar to most CDs, the prepared ME-CDs displayed the excitation-dependent PL behavior. As shown in Fig. 3b, with the increase of excitation wavelength from 300nm to 420nm, the emission wavelength red-shifted gradually. The strongest fluorescence with maximum emission wavelength of 420nm was obtained under the excitation wavelength of 320nm. Although the exact mechanisms of fluorescent properties of carbon dots remained unclear, previous reports confirmed that the excitation-dependent PL behavior is related to size, edge defects and surface state of CDs [20,25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The peroxidase-like activity of the ME-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of the ME-CDs was evaluated by the catalytic oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 4 , the TMB+H2O2 solution was colorless and showed no evident absorbance near 652nm. Upon the addition of the nanodots, the mixture changed to deep blue color and two absorption peaks at 370 and 652nm were observed. The results demonstrated that the ME-CDs possessed high peroxidase-like catalytic activity toward TMB oxidation.

Then catalytic activity of the nanodots was also studied. In view of chemical composition of the nanodots, the catalytic activity could be attributed to two possible reasons. One is graphitic carbon linked with CO group, which has been testified to have intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity [16]. The existence of graphitic carbon and CO has been confirmed by the XPS and FTIR results. Another one is the trace amounts of Fe element in the ME-CDs. It was known that there are high concentration of hemoglobin in animal blood [26], and hemoglobin displayed strong electrochemically catalytic activity to H2O2 due to heme moiety [27]. In addition, it was reported that carbonized hemoglobin nanofibers also possessed electrochemically catalytic activity to H2O2 redox reaction attributed to Fe species [28]. Thus we thought that catalytic activity of the nanodots may be related to hemoglobin in animal blood.

In order to explore the mechanism of the nanodots' catalytic activity, other carbon-based nanodots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of BPEI, citric acid, hemoglobin, BPEI+protoporphyrin, and BPEI+FeCl3 at 180°C. The obtained nanodots were labeled as B-CDs, C-CDs, H-CDs, P-CDs, and Fe-CDs, respectively. Catalytic activity of these nanodots was studied by the TMB-H2O2 reaction as well. As shown in Fig. 4b, only H-CDs and Fe-CDs displayed high catalytic activity to the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. But the CDs prepared from BPEI+protoporphyrin only exhibited weak catalytic capability, and the CDs made from BPEI and citric acid didn’t demonstrate obvious catalytic property. The results demonstrated that the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the ME-CDs was related to the Fe species in the nanodots, and carbon dots prepared from common organic compounds didn’t display evident peroxidase-like catalytic activity.

Although the chemical composition of Fe species in ME-CDs could not be confirmed due to the content of Fe is too low, we suggested that the catalytic activity of the ME-CDs was attributed to Fe species in the nanodots.

Based on the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of ME-CDs, colorimetric detection of glucose was explored. As shown in Fig. 5 , the mixture of ME-CDs+TMB (curve a) and glucose+ME-CDs+TMB (curve b) did not display distinct color change or absorption increase at 652nm. However when glucose was mixed with GOx at 37°C for 20min, the addition of TMB and ME-CDs caused obvious color change and strong UV absorption at 652nm (curve c). The result demonstrated that H2O2 was formed after the incubation process. Based on the theory, a novel glucose sensor can be designed with the prepared ME-CDs.

The mechanism of the colorimetric method towards glucose determination can be described as following (Scheme 1 ). On the surface of ME-CDs, glucose was oxidized to generate gluconolactone and H2O2 in the presence of GOx during incubation process. Subsequently the TMB was oxidized to oxTMB by produced H2O2 under the catalysis of ME-CDs, which caused the system changed blue. Glucose can be quantified based on the theory.

In order to obtain the best efficiency, some reaction buffer pH and reaction time were optimized. As shown in Fig. S4, the best UV–vis absorption response signal to glucose was obtained at pH 4.5. Fig. S4B showed that the absorbance values at 652nm increased gradually after ME-CDs were mixed with glucose and reached balance after 2h.

Glucose was detected by the TMB oxidation under optimized condition. Firstly glucose was mixed with GOx and ME-CDs and incubated for 2h before the addition of TMB. As shown in Fig. 6 , with the increase of glucose concentration, the UV–vis absorption decreased gradually and the absorbance intensity at 652nm was proportional to the concentration of glucose in the range of 0.20–2.5mM (Fig. 5C). The regression equation was A=0.740 c+0.131 with a correlation coefficient of 0.992 and a low detection limit of 0.06mM (S/N=3) for glucose sensing. For colorimetric detection of glucose, the graph in Fig. 5B displayed that 0.2mM glucose could cause obvious color change. In view of that the general range of blood glucose concentration in human blood is about 3–13mM, the proposed method could be applied to detect glucose in real samples [29].

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed sensing strategy, we have performed the TMB oxidation study in the presence of other possible interferences, such as fructose, lactose, maltose and sucrose. From the results in Fig. 7 , it is clear that only glucose induced a dramatic change in the absorption intensity, whereas little changes were observed in the presence of other interference substances. The results clearly indicated that those existing interferences did not affect glucose detection, demonstrating the present method showcased high selectivity toward glucose. The proposed method was applied to detect glucose in different serum samples and the results are shown in Fig. 7B and Table 1 . According to the linear calibration curve mentioned above, glucose in the sample can be calculated to be 5.23mM, 6.35mM and 6.52mM, respectively. The general range of blood glucose concentration in healthy persons is about 3–8mM [27]. In order to verify the accuracy of the method, the same samples were determined with common handheld glucose meter. The results in Table 1 agreed well with the values obtained from handheld glucose meter. In addition, the standard deviation for five measurements was less than 5%, indicating the good reproducibility of the method. Therefore, the proposed method could be applied to detect glucose in real samples.

In summary, multielement-doped carbon dots (ME-CDs) were synthesized by simple hydrothermal treatment of duck blood. The size of as-synthesized ME-CDs was about 2.0–3.5nm with an average diameter of 2.4nm. The resulting ME-CDs displayed excellent fluorescence properties with a fluorescence quantum yield of 32.6%. Results confirmed that the nanodots were mainly nitrogen-doped with trace amount of sulfur and iron elements. In particular, the nanodots exhibited peroxidase-like catalytic activity which is attributed to the iron species doped in the nanodots. In addition, the materials can be used to fabricate colorimetric glucose sensor, demonstrating good sensitivity and specificity. The synthesized ME-CDs could also have great potential to perform as an artificial enzyme, a sensitizer, a florescent reporter and a catalyst to emerge broad applications in biosensors and green energies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Fundamental Research Funds (XDJK2016C087) for the Central Universities and PhD start-up grants SWU113112 from Southwest University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.09.067.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reference ranges, Approach to Anemia in the Adult and Child

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bin Wang get his doctorate from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun. He joined Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials and Technologies of Clean Energies in Southwest University in 2013. His research interests focus on Biosensing, DNA based nanomaterials, and biomedicine.
Feng Liu received his master's degree from Southwest University in 2014. His research focuses on carbon materials based biosensing and biomedicine.
Yuanya Wu received her master's degree from Southwest University in 2016. Her research interest focuses on synthesis of carbon materials and fabrication of paper-based biosensing.
Yanfen Chen received her master's degree from Southwest University in 2016. Her research interests include biosensors and optical assays for nucleic acids and proteins.

Bo Weng get her doctorate from Australian Institute of Innovative Materials in University of Wollongong in 2010. Her research interest focus on electrochemical Biosensor, carbon nanomaterials, and biomedicine.

Dr Chang Ming Li is Professor and Dean of the Institute for Clean Energy and Advanced Materials in Southwest University, China. He worked with Motorola USA (1993–2003) as a distinguished technical member, Science Advisory Board Associate and senior manager. He was Professor, Head of Bioengineering Division and Director of Centre for Advanced Bionanosystems in Nanyang Technological University, Singapore (2003–2012). His main research interests are nanomaterials, green energies and biosensors/lab-on-a chip. He is serving as an Associate Editor of RSC Advances and as a member of the Advisory Board for Nanoscale, RSC.
